response_parsed.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: It makes me quite uneasy, this whole business of the gender gap in science. When I was younger, back when things were simpler, you just went to school and you studied what you liked, didn't you? But seeing how it is now, with all this talk on the television and the internet, it seems like some things are just… set up wrong. I don't understand all the complicated science, not with my schooling, but I see things, you know, when I read these novels or watch the news. It feels like there's a wall, a bit of a barrier, that women find when they want to get into these big labs or universities. And honestly, I think we must address it, because if we aren't fair, then we are missing out on some important things for the whole country, for everyone. It's just common sense, really. How to fix it? Well, I don't have a fancy university degree to tell you the proper way, but from what I see, maybe it starts small. Perhaps in the schools, when the young ones are learning, the teachers need to make sure they are showing examples of women doing great things in science, not just the usual men. And maybe, in those big institutions, they need to look at how they treat people. Are the working hours too hard? Are the opportunities spread out fairly? Sometimes, when I watch these dramas on cinema, the characters face unfairness, and it makes me anxious, because I see that same pattern in real life. It shouldn't be like that. We just need things to be more balanced, more sensible, so that talent, whatever it comes from, can just shine through without all this fuss and worry.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an 81-year-old retired Italian man with lower secondary education, high conscientiousness, and high neuroticism. His tone should be anxious, reflective, somewhat traditional, and grounded in common sense rather than academic theory. He will express unease about the issue, advocate for addressing it based on a sense of fairness, and propose simple, relatable solutions (like better role models in schools or fair workplace practices) rather than complex policy changes. The language will be simple and conversational, reflecting his limited formal education.
response_parsed.tone: Anxious, reflective, and grounded in common sense
